ManifestoOne-pagerInvestment memo

Molecule War

The greatest energy renaissance since oil has begun. Lavoit will own it.

Short on time? Read the one-pager: the TL;DR of this manifesto.

01

Cost is law.

Civilization compounds on cheap energy, not on announcements, mandates, or moral preference. Energy in the right form, at the lowest cost, is what turns invention into infrastructure. When energy gets cheaper, everything downstream gets larger: food, transport, industry, compute, cities.

In 1859 Edwin Drake drilled 69 feet into the ground in Titusville, Pennsylvania. Eleven years later Rockefeller founded Standard Oil. Within two decades he controlled 75% of the global kerosene trade. Kerosene did not beat whale oil because the world became enlightened. It beat whale oil because it was cheaper, more abundant, and easier to move.

Baku told the same story a generation later, and Shell after that. The pattern never changed: make energy cheaper in the form civilization already needs, then take the market.

Ford did not make oil important. Cheap oil made Ford inevitable. The Model T went from $850 to $260 because Ford crushed manufacturing time from 12.5 hours to 93 minutes. When the fuel is cheap and the machine is cheap, adoption stops being a debate and becomes gravity.

Since 1860 the world grew from 1.3 billion people to 8.3 billion. Real GDP multiplied roughly 60×. That was not powered by slogans, but by molecules pulled from the ground at scale and sold cheaper than every other source. The next energy company wins by the same law, or it does not win.

02

The hard majority.

When you reverse-engineer the global energy system there is one fact that cuts through the noise: only 21% of final energy is consumed as electricity. The remaining 79% is the molecule system. The fuels you burn, the heat you move, the feedstocks you transform, the materials you build with.

The 79% is not one market. It splits into two different systems. Combusted molecules are roughly 60% of final energy: industrial heat, heavy transport, building heat, aviation, shipping, dispatchable power. These molecules are used once: brought to a chamber, oxidized, part of the heat turned into work and the rest thrown away.

Material molecules are roughly 15%: steel reduction, olefins, feedstocks, bitumen, lubricants, ammonia, methanol, hydrogen. These molecules are not just burned. They are transformed into food, steel, plastics, chemicals, roads, and the physical base of civilization.

That split is the map that matters. Combustion is the incumbent’s strongest position because the route is short: pump the gas, burn the gas. Material molecules are different. They pass through long process chains, and every conversion along the way adds its own inefficiency and cost. That is where the system starts to crack open.

03

Every conversion is a tax.

Reverse-engineer the molecule system and the same pattern keeps appearing: every conversion is a tax. Extraction pays it long before the customer sees the molecule. Gas is drilled, cleaned, compressed, sometimes liquefied, shipped, regasified, and burned; coal is mined, washed, hauled, pulverised, and burned to boil water; oil is lifted, distilled, cracked, reformed, hydrotreated, blended, and distributed. The cost isn’t only in the atom; it’s in the process.

Combustion is the incumbent’s home turf.

When a molecule is only burned, the incumbent route already sits close to its source: natural gas can be extracted, moved, and burned, and geology did the synthesis for free. The route is short, so the conversion tax is low. Routing that same heat through hydrogen adds steps instead of removing them.

Fossil combustion
Extract→Process→Move→Burn
Hydrogen combustion
Electricity + water→Hydrogen→Process→Move→Burn

The two routes are about the same length, and the fossil one is already built. That is why combustion is the last thing to fall, not the first.

Materials are where the route gets long.

Material molecules are transformed, not just burned, and every transformation is another tax. Ammonia and methanol are built by forcing hydrogen onto nitrogen or carbon; steel is made by using hydrogen to strip the oxygen out of iron ore, the job coal does cheaply today. Different products, same core input — and today that input is made from natural gas.

Ammonia via natural gas (today)
Extract→Process→Move→Reform→Hydrogen→Compress→Synthesise→Chill→Ammonia
Ammonia via electrolysis
Electricity + water→Hydrogen→Synthesise→Ammonia

Same product, a fraction of the chain. Every deleted step is a conversion tax the incumbent pays and we don’t. Materials are where cheap electrolytic hydrogen has a structural cost advantage, so materials fall first.

Every long route already runs through hydrogen.

The incumbents don’t use natural gas because gas is sacred. They use it because gas was the cheapest path to hydrogen. The expensive middle step of the material economy is already hydrogen. Make hydrogen cheaper than the incumbent route, and the whole chain shifts toward the cheaper input.

So the system reduces to one number.

The closer a molecule stays to its source, the cheaper it is. Stay at hydrogen when the application allows; do one synthesis step when the market requires it; don’t recreate extraction complexity unless the customer has no lower-cost option. Strip every route in the energy system to its components and one parameter is left standing: the cost of hydrogen. Make it cheap enough and material molecules fall first, because that is where extraction pays the most taxes; combustion becomes reachable later, when the learning curve crosses gas.

04

Cheap hydrogen.

The goal is one number: make hydrogen so cheap it outcompetes hydrogen made from natural gas, and eventually natural gas itself once the learning curve has done its work. Everything downstream of this page depends on that number. You don’t cut a cost by wanting it lower; you cut it by understanding what it’s made of today, and how it got this way.

The price to beat is gas.

Start with what exists. Hydrogen from natural gas (steam methane reforming) lands at roughly $0.7–2/kg depending on where the gas is: around $0.7/kg in Saudi Arabia, $1.4–1.9/kg across Europe. That is the price to beat.

Electrolytic hydrogen isn’t close.

Electrolytic hydrogen is nowhere near it. On paper, hydrogen from an incumbent machine lands at $5–7/kg. Reality is worse: every plant is sold as a bespoke, non-turnkey engineering project, and the EPC firms that integrate it eat roughly another 30% of the cost. Most companies we have talked to hold $5/kg as a 2030 goal. The cheapest real number we have heard on European grid power is Stegra’s $2.75/kg, still above the expensive end of gas. The machine is the anchor: a European alkaline system runs about $2,400/kW installed, and even China’s stripped-down versions land around $540/kW. A delivered kilogram splits three ways: the electricity that goes in, the capital cost of the machine, and the upkeep. On grid power, electricity is 65–70% of the delivered cost.

The machine is a century-old inheritance.

How did the machine get this expensive? Its history. Alkaline electrolyzers have run since the 1920s, parked next to hydropower dams to make ammonia where power happened to be cheap. In the 1960s General Electric built the PEM cell for NASA: the customer was a spacecraft, the spec was small and efficient at any price, and cost per kilogram was never in it. At system level PEM still does not decisively beat the century-old alkaline machine.

Every machine since inherited the assumptions of the world it was born into: electricity is expensive, and the machine runs static on a grid. Process-industry doctrine finished the job: a plant turns on, runs flat, and stays running until maintenance. It is what engineers are taught in school, what the models assume, and what every incumbent electrolyzer is designed around.

Efficiency was never the lever.

I know that lock-in personally, because I grew up inside it. I started building electrolyzers at twelve, in a lab I built in my grandparents’ old hen house. I built for efficiency, because everyone built for efficiency. My first stack hit 70%. The next hit 90%, with catalysts, and died in a day from catalyst degradation. I was optimizing exactly the variable the incumbents had taught me to optimize, and it took a dead stack to see it: efficiency was never the lever. Delivered cost is.

The incumbents keep chasing efficiency anyway, and the spiral feeds itself. Expensive grid electricity → chase efficiency → exotic catalysts and years of R&D → an expensive, fragile machine → expensive hydrogen → few units sold → the R&D bill spread across fewer units → an even more expensive machine. More than $200 billion has been announced for hydrogen projects. Delivered output, as of mid-2026, is close to zero relative to the promise.

We verified it from the inside.

We verified this from the inside. Before building anything, we spoke with more than 80% of the operating electrolyzer sites on Earth: visited plants, ran diagnostics, and saw the operating data that never reaches a conference slide. In Rotterdam we brought leaders and CEOs from thyssenkrupp nucera, Sunfire, ITM Power, BASF and Repsol around one dinner table and a few glasses down they started giving the real info on manufacturing, China, degradation, cost, and project failures. The conclusion was blunt: the people running the incumbents do not understand the machines they sell. They cannot tell you where their cost sits, why their stacks degrade, or why their projects slip. They inherited a machine, a price, and a doctrine, and they have never questioned any of it.

And for 99% of today’s hydrogen business, none of this matters, because nobody is trying to win. It is a nine-to-five industry: collect the clean-hydrogen grants, build a showcase electrolyzer, publish the press release, and be happy with that. Without the ambition to take over the world, nobody has had a reason to rebuild the machine in a hundred years. The doctrine survives because nobody needs it to die.

The real lever is the cheapest electron.

If electricity is 70% of the cost, the move is to take the cheapest electron on Earth, whatever the machine has to become. For a century that logic pointed at natural gas because it was such a cheap path to hydrogen that you did not need an electrolyzer at all. That era is ending. The cheapest electron on Earth now comes from solar.

Solar have had the most ruthless learning curve in energy. Module prices have fallen 99.6% since 1976, with cost dropping another 20.2% for every doubling of installed capacity. It is already the cheapest electricity on Earth, and it is still getting cheaper.

Put the two levers side by side. A 5% efficiency gain on $50/MWh grid power saves about $0.15/kg of hydrogen. Moving from $50/MWh grid power to $8.57/MWh direct solar saves about $2.50/kg. The electricity lever is 17× larger than the efficiency lever. Efficiency matters, but cost is what decides.

Physics sets the floor; the machine is the gap.

Physics puts a floor under all of this. Splitting water (H₂O → H₂ + ½O₂) takes 286 kJ/mol, about 39 kWh per kilogram of hydrogen, at a reversible voltage of 1.23 V; catalysts trim the losses but cannot touch the bond energy, and every millivolt a real stack runs above that becomes heat. At $8.57/MWh solar, that floor is about $0.34 of electricity per kilogram of hydrogen. The physics was never the expensive part: the gap between that floor and today’s $5–7/kg is the machine.

Except the incumbent machine cannot take the gift. Put a $2,400/kW electrolyzer on solar and its hydrogen gets more expensive, not cheaper: electricity nearly disappears from the cost and the machine takes over. The incumbent machine is built wrong for the cheapest electricity on Earth.

Solar breaks the doctrine.

The incumbents have watched this curve for a decade and still refused it, because solar breaks the doctrine. Solar is dynamic: it ramps every morning, dies every evening, and swings with the weather. Process engineers are trained to treat that as disqualifying: plants run static. The fear underneath is degradation, real but specific: load cycling kills catalyst layers, so the degradation problem is really a catalyst problem.

Take that to its conclusion. If God gives you the cheapest source of electrons on Earth, you adapt the machine to fit them, not the other way around. Delete the catalysts: they are manufacturing complexity that buys marginal efficiency, and on sub-$10/MWh power that margin is not worth its cost or its fragility. Design the stack to ramp with the sun and sit idle at night. Accept 20–30% utilization. Couple directly to the field: no grid connection, no rectifier stack, no utility engineering, none of the years and millions that interconnection burns. Then the entire cost problem lands in one place: capex.

Nobody built the machine solar needs.

So the unlock is simple. Cheap solar does not need an efficient electrolyzer; it needs a cheap one: a machine with capex so low that running 2,200 hours a year beats running 8,000. The incumbents sit north of $2,000/kW and would need to fall roughly thirty-fold to make that math work. Nobody built that machine. So we did.

05

Jörmungandr.

We named the machine after Jörmungandr, the Norse world-serpent — so vast it wraps the entire earth and grips its own tail. The name is the ambition in one word: a single machine, numbered up without end, coiled around the planet, making the molecules civilization runs on.

Rank every part by idiot index.

The engineering started long before any plant existed. We tore the electrolyzer apart on paper and ranked every component by idiot index: how far its finished price sits above its raw material and ordinary manufacturing cost. Cheap was only half the specification: every part also had to be easy to manufacture, transport, and install, so the machine commissions in days, not years.

Build, break, benchmark.

We built three test benches, built and broke our own small stacks, and bought incumbent stacks to tear down and benchmark. By the time the machine existed on paper, its cost structure was mapped.

Boring where boring wins.

The architecture is deliberately boring where boring wins. Zero-gap alkaline. Bare nickel foam electrodes. 30 wt% KOH at 80–90°C. Atmospheric operation below PED threshold. 70×70 cm active-area cells. No fragile miracle layer.

The breakthrough is cost engineering, not chemistry.

The cost breakthrough is cost engineering at the component level rather than exotic chemistry. Bipolar plates go from machined stainless to PP-GF30 plastic with stamped nickel current collectors: hours on a CNC machine become seconds in an injection mold, and most of the stack cost goes with them.

Take two Nords 316L stainless nipples with the same function: one CNC-machined from hexagon bar, one cut from commodity tube and threaded. Same alloy, same manufacturer, same connection, and six times the price. The difference isn’t the metal; it’s how it was made.

Illustrative procurement check: Nords International Sweden AB 316L / 1.4404 stainless nipples and a standard PP-H 90° elbow. Prices rounded and used to show cost structure, not as a live quote.
Attack the highest idiot index first.

Nickel, polypropylene, stainless: none of them are expensive. What gets expensive is forcing cheap atoms through slow, bespoke, high-touch process. So we attack the highest idiot index first: PP-H socket-fusion piping instead of stainless, commodity pressure vessels for gas-liquid separation and mag-drive pumps, in-house dry coolers.

Then we built it.

Then we built it. A 1 MW electrolyzer system, six weeks from first P&ID drawing to commissioned site. Four people, end to end. All-in cost (electrical installation, materials, manufacturing): $120/kW.

The build was the education.

The build was the education. I learned more in those six weeks than in the six months since. We touched every part of the system, so every mistake is mapped: CNC machines breaking mid-run, producing cell components at scale, soldering connectors, flow channels, getting electricity to the site on time. Outsource that work and you learn nothing, and make the same mistakes at 50 MW with customers and capital attached.

The sharpest lesson: components are easy, process is hard. Every part was written down, almost none of the process, and every missing instruction became a person, a phone call, a delay. The balance of plant could be assembled in three days; without the process on paper it took two people almost two weeks. With the assembly written as a manual, the same build runs first drawing to commissioning in under a month.

Heroic effort finds truth, not a company.

We ran on heroic founder effort. It finds truth; it does not run a company. So we rebuilt ours around the gaps the build exposed: ownership, documentation, manufacturing routes, sequencing, purchasing, QC.

Scale by repeating one stack.

The 1 MW stack was our test system: same geometry, same piping, same pumps, same cooling that carries to every scale. The 5 MW commercial module is five identical stacks in one container, steel traded for plastic where it carries no load, so it ships road-legal on a normal truck. We get bigger by repeating that one stack, never by building a bigger machine. The 5 MW commercial system operates in September, targeting sub-$60/kW, a waypoint rather than an end state.

Put it on the cheapest electron.

Put that machine on the cheapest electron on Earth and the cost table stops being theoretical.

ParameterS1 · EU grid + Inc.S2 · Solar + Inc.S3 · EU grid + LavoitS4 · Solar + Lavoit
Electrolyzer CAPEX$2,400/kW$2,400/kW$60/kW$60/kW
Electricity price$50/MWh$8.57/MWh$50/MWh$8.57/MWh
Full-load hours8,000 hr/yr2,200 hr/yr8,000 hr/yr2,200 hr/yr
Degradation1.5%/yr2.0%/yr0.6%/yr0.6%/yr
OPEX$42/kW/yr$42/kW/yr$1.2/kW/yr$1.2/kW/yr
LCOH$5.16/kg$7.60/kg$3.23/kg$0.74/kg
Scenario 2 shows why incumbents die on solar: high CAPEX + low capacity factor = worst-case economics. Lavoit’s low CAPEX is what makes the cheapest electricity on Earth usable. WACC 7%, 25-year project life.
Geography turns into hydrogen cost.

Cheap solar is not evenly distributed, so neither is cheap hydrogen. Screen the planet for coastal sites with brutal, stable sun (within 10 km of a coast, above 1,900 kWh/m²/year) and 3,747 sites in 65 countries qualify, with a median site proxy around $0.83/kg once local solar shape is included. The coast filter is logistics: seawater in, because these plants need water, and shipping routes out, because the product goes straight onto a ship instead of waiting for rail and pipeline infrastructure to be built. Those sites are the new oil fields: geography turning into hydrogen cost.

06

Lavoit engineering.

Jörmungandr was only feasible because three founders each carried a piece of the machine in their heads. That does not scale. What we scale on instead is the hygiene and structure we built from those lessons: sharper engineering across the stack and everything around it, then a factory that mass-produces the technologies, the foundation the largest company in the universe gets built on.

Production layout: stack production (injection molding, stamping, fiber and CO₂ laser, robotic assembly), tank production, piping production, and final assembly with AGVs and an overhead crane.

In the factory the stack is built by its own machine (injection molding, stamping, fiber and CO₂ laser cutting, robotic assembly), and the 5 MW module is designed so five people assemble it in under two hours. The line is 3,000 m² and costs $50M. Construction starts next year, once all the modules are running.

The line will grow. The first one makes the stack, the piping, and the tanks; then we take another component in-house, month by month, in order of idiot index. Every component we pull in deletes another vendor’s 20% margin: capex falls, and the learning rate from mass production cuts it again.

The machine is not the business. We deploy the electrolyzers with solar all around the world, own the assets, and sell the molecules that ship out. We own everything from photon to molecule.

The essence of Lavoit is three by three:

  • Develop three technologies.The electrolyzer. The synthesis reactor. The fuel cell.
  • Mass-deploy three things.Solar. The machines. The logistics infrastructure.
  • Conquer three molecules.Ammonia first. Then methanol and the olefins. Lastly, combustion.
07

The moat.

Every empire gets asked the same question: what stops the others? Standard Oil answered with cost, scale, and logistics. Ours is the same answer, four layers deep.

The incumbents cannot follow without dying. Their machines, their doctrine, and their installed base are engineered for expensive grid power running flat-out. To copy us they must first admit the century-old machine is wrong, obsolete their own product line, and rebuild their factories around a $60/kW architecture that destroys their margins. They have watched the solar curve for a decade and refused it. Institutions do not commit suicide to chase a startup.

The knowledge does not transfer. We built Jörmungandr with our own hands: every mistake mapped, every process written into manuals. Components are easy; process is hard. A competitor can buy our machine and tear it down, and they will find cheap plastic and bare nickel: the same atoms that have been available to everyone for a hundred years. What they cannot tear down is the route: the manufacturing sequence, the assembly manuals, the supplier map, the thousand decisions that turn $60/kW from a slide into a commissioned site. That lives in us.

The lead compounds. Every component pulled into the factory deletes a vendor’s 20% margin. Every doubling of production cuts cost again through the learning rate. A follower who starts today starts at our year-zero cost while we ride the curve down. In manufacturing, a first mover’s lead usually widens instead of closing, the way Ford’s did.

Geography gets claimed once. 3,747 sites qualify. The best sun, the best coasts, the best ports are finite, and the first mover signs the land, the water, and the offtake. When our molecule sets the price floor at $0.74/kg, entering the market means beating a machine you do not have, on fields you cannot get, against a cost curve that started falling years before you arrived.

The moat isn’t one wall; it’s four, and each one grows.

Endgame.

The war ends quietly. One contract at a time, our molecule lands under theirs, and the market crosses over without a speech. Nobody switches back. A well depletes; a factory only compounds.

Then energy is no longer found. It is made from sun, air, and water, priced by a learning curve instead of a cartel, falling forever. Whoever manufactures the floor of civilization owns everything built on top of it.

This is Lavoit. Molecules are not our last war. War is just the beginning.

Read the investment memo.
